QF.id: Terminology in Equity and Debt Markets_C2_4

question.text: Scenario: Vikram Desai, a fixed income analyst, is evaluating two corporate bonds for inclusion in his portfolio: Bond A — Issued by Infra Corp Ltd: Face Value Rs.1,000, Coupon Rate 9% payable annually, Current Market Price Rs.950, Remaining maturity 5 years. Bond B — Issued by Tech Solutions Ltd: Face Value Rs.1,000, Coupon Rate 7.5% payable annually, Current Market Price Rs.1,050, Remaining maturity 5 years, Callable at Rs.1,020 after 3 years. Both bonds have similar credit ratings (AA+). Vikram needs to analyze these bonds to make his investment recommendation. Question 4 of 5: The callable feature of Bond B at Rs.1,020 after 3 years means:

answer.text: Vikram can sell the bond back to the issuer at Rs.1,020
The issuer can redeem the bond at Rs.1,020 after 3 years if interest rates fall, limiting Vikram's upside
The bond automatically converts to equity after 3 years
The coupon rate increases to Rs.1,020 after 3 years

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The callable feature gives the ISSUER the right to redeem the bond at Rs.1,020 after 3 years. If rates fall, the issuer will call the bond and refinance at lower rates, limiting the bondholder's price appreciation potential. **The Trap:** A put feature would give Vikram the right to sell back. Callable is the issuer's option, not the investor's. It does not convert to equity. **Cross-Exam Context:** Call risk is an important concept in NISM Series XV and IV. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When evaluating callable bonds, calculate Yield to Call in addition to YTM. The lower of the two is the more relevant measure.
